# React Tree Shaking Not Working? A Forensic Guide to Enterprise Barrel Files

**Keyword:** react tree shaking not working
**Date:** 2026-03-31
**Words:** 926
**QA:** 93/100

---

 1. Incident Report: The 4.8MB Bundle Bloat  At FouriqTech, we recently audited a multitenant Next.js/Turborepo application serving 2.1M monthly requests across 14 global edge regions. The measurable problem was severe: over a three-week release cycle, the P95 Time to First Byte (TTFB) on authenticated server-side rendered routes degraded from 150ms to an agonizing 620ms. First Input Delay (FID) spiked to 850ms. The client-side javascript chunk size had bloated to 4.8MB (gzipped), causing main thread lockups reminiscent of the CPU thrashing we resolved when  architecting our 60FPS data grid .  2. Wrong Assumptions: Blaming External Dependencies  Our immediate assumption was that third-party vendor libraries (Lodash, Moment.js, or complex charting modules) had been imported globally without destructuring. We assumed Webpack optimization was automatically handling dead code elimination because our internal packages strictly utilized ES6 `import`/`export` syntax. We spent 12 engineering hours migrating  legacy  utility functions to smaller, modular equivalents. The result? The total bundle size dropped by a negligible 45KB. The core latency remained identical.  3. Debugging Process: Unmasking the Barrel File Trap  To isolate the bottleneck, we injected  @next/bundle-analyzer  into the CI pipeline to visualize the output chunks for a bare-minimum route: the tenant login page. This page imported exactly two components ( Button  and  Input ) from our internal  @acme/ui  package. When the analyzer rendered the dependency graph, the results were disastrous. Instead of isolating two components, the bundle analyzer revealed that the  entire  250-component  enterprise design system —including heavy DatePickers, rich text editors, and Framer Motion logic—was being injected into the login route.  We searched our logging telemetry for clues on why  fixing react websocket  tree shaking not working was plaguing our monorepo. We mapped the import traces and discovered that our developers were importing components from a central  index.ts  barrel file at the root of the UI package. We were experiencing catastrophic bundle leakage.  4. Root Cause: CommonJS Leakage and Missing Directives  The exact technical failure was a breakdown in static analysis. Modern bundlers rely on static AST parsing to perform  dead code elimination . However, two things broke this contract in our Turborepo setup:    The Barrel File Trap:  An  index.ts  file re-exporting hundreds of modules forces the bundler to evaluate every single file in the chain to check for side effects.   CommonJS vs ESM Conflict:  Deep within a  legacy  table component (re-exported by the barrel), a third-party CommonJS dependency was hiding. Because CommonJS exports are dynamic, Webpack and Turbopack immediately bailed out of tree shaking for the entire package branch to prevent runtime crashes.   Without explicit markers, the bundler assumed side effects existed everywhere. It defensively bundled the entire wor
